coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal synthesis and efficient production of H2O2 using Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst subject to carbon quantum dots loading

coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as promising co-catalysts in photocatalysis owing to their superior light-harvesting capabilities, tunable surface chemistry, and eco-compatibility. This work demonstrates a rationally designed CQDs/Bi2MoO6 (BMO) composite material synthesized via hydrothermal methods for solar-driven hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) production. By precisely optimizing CQDs loading ratios, the composite achieves an 11.75-fold enhancement in H2O2 generation rate compared to pristine BMO within 2 h, while maintaining 94.66 % activity retention over multiple cycles. Systematic characterization reveals three synergistic mechanisms: 1) CQDs broaden visible-light absorption through antenna effects and facilitate interfacial charge separation via Schottky junctions; 2) Optimized energy band alignment between CQDs and BMO simultaneously satisfies thermodynamic requirements for oxygen reduction (ORR: O2 → H2O2) and water oxidation (WOR: H2O → O2) reactions; 3) Abundant edge-active sites on CQDs promote *OOH intermediate stabilization during H₂O₂ formation. Notably, the study identifies a critical CQDs loading threshold (6 wt%) beyond which excessive surface coverage induces carrier recombination and disrupts reaction interfaces, causing performance decline. These findings establish a quantitative structure-activity-stability relationship for hybrid photocatalysts, addressing longstanding challenges in balancing co-catalyst dispersion and functional integrity. The work not only provides fundamental insights into interfacial engineering of quantum dot-semiconductor systems but also proposes a scalable synthetic strategy for robust solar-to-chemical conversion technologies.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal synthesis and efficient production of H2O2 using Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst subject to carbon quantum dots loading

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanics and Photoelectric Physics, Anhui University of Science and Technology, Huainan 232001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanics and Photoelectric Physics
Anhui University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanics and Photoelectric Physics, Anhui University of Science and Technology, Huainan 232001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as promising co-catalysts in photocatalysis owing to their superior light-harvesting capabilities, tunable surface chemistry, and eco-compatibility. This work demonstrates a rationally designed CQDs/Bi2MoO6 (BMO) composite material synthesized via hydrothermal methods for solar-driven hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) production. By precisely optimizing CQDs loading ratios, the composite achieves an 11.75-fold enhancement in H2O2 generation rate compared to pristine BMO within 2 h, while maintaining 94.66 % activity retention over multiple cycles. Systematic characterization reveals three synergistic mechanisms: 1) CQDs broaden visible-light absorption through antenna effects and facilitate interfacial charge separation via Schottky junctions; 2) Optimized energy band alignment between CQDs and BMO simultaneously satisfies thermodynamic requirements for oxygen reduction (ORR: O2 → H2O2) and water oxidation (WOR: H2O → O2) reactions; 3) Abundant edge-active sites on CQDs promote *OOH intermediate stabilization during H₂O₂ formation. Notably, the study identifies a critical CQDs loading threshold (6 wt%) beyond which excessive surface coverage induces carrier recombination and disrupts reaction interfaces, causing performance decline. These findings establish a quantitative structure-activity-stability relationship for hybrid photocatalysts, addressing longstanding challenges in balancing co-catalyst dispersion and functional integrity. The work not only provides fundamental insights into interfacial engineering of quantum dot-semiconductor systems but also proposes a scalable synthetic strategy for robust solar-to-chemical conversion technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a crucial chemical raw material and oxidizing agent with a broad applications pharmaceuticals, environmental protection, papermaking and textiles [1–3]. Conventional production methods such as the anthraquinone process and palladium-catalyzed routes, rely on toxic solvents and explosive hydrogen precursors, leading to high energy consumption and environmental risks [4]. Photocatalytic H2O2 synthesis offers a green alternative by utilizing sunlight to drive oxygen reduction (ORR) and water oxidation reactions (WOR), with advantages including mild conditions, renewable energy utilization, and negligible byproducts [5,6]. However, challenges like low light absorption efficiency, rapid charge recombination, and insufficient active sites hinder its practical adoption [7,8].

Bismuth molybdate (Bi2MoO6, BMO) is a promising semiconductor for photocatalysis due to its direct bandgap, visible-light absorption, and layered Bi-O/Mo-O structure. As an n-type semiconductor material, its reduction reactions are highly efficient, with a high concentration of free electrons in the conduction band, making it suitable for participating in reduction reactions such as H2O2, H2 production, and CO2 reduction [5,9–14]. Despite its direct bandgap facilitating electron-hole separation in theory, experimental studies reveal persistent recombination losses attributed to interlayer charge migration barriers and surface defect trapping, limiting its practical efficiency [15]. Compared to UV-limited catalysts like TiO2 and ZnO, BMO's visible-light activity is advantageous but remains suboptimal due to insufficient active site exposure and sluggish reaction kinetics [16,17]. Recent efforts to modify BMO include heterojunctions, metal doping, and defect engineering, yet these strategies often involve complex synthesis or toxic components, compromising scalability and sustainability [18–20].

Photocatalytic H2O2 production using BMO has been advanced through strategies like oxygen vacancy engineering [21], heterojunction [22], metal doping [23], and metal−organic frameworks (MOFs) composites [24], yet these methods often involve complex synthesis. Surface modification with quantum dots (QDs) offers a simpler alternative. While metallic QDs (CdSe, SnO2, etc.) show promise in H2O2 generation, their toxicity, high cost, and environmental risks limit scalability [4,25–28]. In contrast, carbon quantum dots (CQDs)—synthesized from abundant precursors—exhibit tunable size, surface functionality, and optical properties (e.g., up conversion photoluminescence), enabling versatile applications [29–37]. CQDs' hybrid amorphous/crystalline carbon cores and oxygen-rich groups enhance active sites, electron storage, and light absorption, synergizing with semiconductors like BMO to suppress charge recombination [38–40]. Prior studies demonstrate CQDs' efficacy in pollutant degradation: boron-doped CQDs/BMO fully degraded BPA in 120 min (vs. 84 % for pure BMO) [41], while CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 achieved 97 % tetracycline removal in 18 min (vs. 37 %) [42]. However, H2O2-focused research remains scarce. Guan et al.'s CPDs/Bi/β-Bi2O3 showed only a 2× H2O2 yield increase [43], likely overshadowed by metallic Bi's dominance, while Hao et al.'s GQDs/BMO treated H2O2 as a degradation byproduct [44]. A systematic exploration of CQDs/BMO for H2O2 production—addressing mechanisms, dual-channel ORR/WOR synergy, and optimal CQDs loading—is critically lacking.

In this study, CQDs/BMO composites were hydrothermally synthesized with controlled CQDs loading, achieving up to 11.9× higher H2O2 production rates than pure BMO. Structural and photoelectrochemical characterization, combined with Mott-Schottky, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR), and radical trapping studies, revealed that CQDs enhance charge separation, optimize band alignment, and accelerate reaction kinetics. This work provides a scalable strategy for designing efficient photocatalysts and mechanistic insights for solar-driven H2O2 synthesis.

The drugs used were citric acid (CA, C6H8O7), ethylenediamine (EDA, C2H8N2), ethylene glycol (EG, C2H6O2), ethanol (EA, C2H6O), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), and ammonium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O). The reagent for the production tests were potassium hydrogen phthalate (KHP, C8H5KO4) and potassium iodide (KI). Radical sacrificial agents were p-benzoquinone (BQ, C6H4O2), isopropyl alcohol (IPA, C3H8O), ammonium oxalate (AO, (NH4)2C2O4), and potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7). All the pharmaceuticals were obtained from Shanghai Maclean Chemical Reagent Company and purity specifications of chemical reagents were analytical reagent (AR).

The synthesis process is depicted in Fig. 1(a). CA was used as the carbon source in 10 mL deionized water (DI H2O), mixing with 335 μL EDA. The mixture was treated by 0.5 h stirred and sonicated each, then through 5 h hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C. Following the function of osmosis membrane (MD44–3500), the cooled solution was dialyzed in DI H2O for 24 h, with replacing the water every 6 h during this period. At last, the product was freeze-dried to obtain CQDs via 3 h freezing and 24 h drying.

Poured 10 mL of EG to a 100 mL beaker, dissolved 0.477 g Bi(NO3)3 and 0.119 g Na2MoO4 following sonication for 0.5 h. Then x mg CQDs (x represents wt%) and 60 mL EA were added to the resulting solution. After 24 h hydrothermal reaction at 160 °C, use DI H2O and EA alternately to wash at least 3 times in a centrifuge. Finally, it was dried normally and grinded in the mortar, which got the products recorded as CxBMO (x = 5, 6, 7 and 9). The above process is described in Fig. 1(b). Similarly, the pure BMO without CQDs was synthesized using the same method.

Testing instrument models are provided in parentheses corresponding to the characterization. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Smartlab) characterized the crystal structure through Cu-Kα radiation, providing the insights of internal atoms for spatial distribution and crystal formation patterns. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific™ ESCALAB™ Xi+) was applied to assess the atomic valence states and surface energy distribution. Raman spectroscopy (Laser microscopic confocal Raman spectrometer, InVia Qontor) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet is50) were utilized to characterize and quantify substances, which obtained the vital information of chemical bonds and functional groups. The absorbance of the material at various wavelengths was determined by ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV–vis, PE Lambda 950). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Talos F200i S) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss Sigma 300) were employed to study the particle structure and composition. Photoluminescence (PL, Edinburgh FLS1000) spectroscopy provided a rapid and straightforward assessment of the luminescent properties of semiconductor materials. Additionally, electron paramagnetic resonance (BRUKE EMXPLUS) signals were recorded to get the relative content of radicals under both dark and illuminated conditions.

For electrochemical experiments, a CHI650B electrochemical analyzer was employed to establish a measurement circuit. Poured 32 μL Nafion and 1.5 mL IPA into a test tube, with mixing sample of 20 mg. The resulting solution was evenly spread onto the pretreated FTO glass' conductive surface (with an area of 200 mm2) and the working electrode was formed through air drying. Preparing 80 mL aqueous solution to make electrolyte by dissolving 2.5208 g of Na2SO4. In the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements, initial voltage was set at 1.55 V, with frequency ranging from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz. Mott-Schottky measurements were conducted at three progressive frequencies, which were 1 kHz, 5 kHz, and 10 kHz. Transient photocurrent response was performed with the 0.7 V initial voltage and 0.1 s sample interval.

During the photocatalytic synthesis of H2O2 experiments, 20 mg catalyst was added to 50 mL DI H2O and the concentration of H2O2 was measured according to iodine titration (Eq. (1)) using 300 W xenon lamp to simulate sunlight. Keeping air conditioning on to maintain the temperature of the reaction area at around 25° Celsius, the reaction solution was stirred at a constant speed and positioned 25 cm away from the light source. Every 30 min, 4 mL of the solution was sampled and 0.22 μm pinhole filters were used to eliminate the interference from catalyst. Then 1 mL solution was drawn into the test tube, mixing 3 mL 0.1 mol L−1 KHP and 3 mL 0.4 mol L−1 KI, which stored in the dark for 30 min. After centrifuging to get the supernatant, measured the absorbance of I3 − by UV–visible spectrophotometry (UVmini-1280, Shimadzu) and the concentration of H2O2 was calculated by the formulae obtained from the calibration curve [45]. Collected the remaining catalyst from the filter and reaction solution, then centrifuged, washed, and dried them for subsequent cyclic testing. Similarly, filter ultraviolet light via loading a 400 nm filter on the xenon lamp and add radical sacrificial agents to conduct other experiments.

Use XRD to analyze the crystal phase composition of CQDs and all catalysts. As shown in Fig. 2(a), a distinct peak at 23.57° corresponds to CQDs' (002) lattice spacing, which is a characteristic peak of amorphous carbon materials [46]. In Fig. 2(b), qualitative analysis of CQDs/BMO composites with varying proportions was performed using MDI Jade 6 software. The phase with the highest matching rate corresponds to the Bi2MoO6 (PDF#72-1524). The main diffraction peaks located at 28.25°, 32.61°, 47.15°, 55.56°, and 58.43° can be retrieved to the (131), (002), (062), (133), and (262) crystal planes respectively. Additionally, the (060) and (191) crystal planes located at 33.1° and 56.2° are clearly visible only in C6BMO and C7BMO. This phenomenon may be attributed to the synthesis process of the composite samples, where CQDs were added directly into the BMO precursor solution instead of being introduced into pre-synthesized BMO. Since CQDs contain a many oxygen-containing functional groups, their nanosheet's surface modified may influence the strength of chemical bonds during the self-assembly of BMO microspheres, slightly affecting the crystal structure of the composite samples [12]. In the meanwhile, the CQDs (002) peak at 23.57° can also be detected in these two samples, albeit with very low intensity. Correspondingly, the crystal structures of other composite samples, where CQDs were not prominently detected, remain consistent with pure BMO. The XRD spectra of the prepared CQDs and CQDs/BMO indicate that the modification of a small or excessive amount of CQDs on the BMO surface does not alter the BMO crystal phase. The appropriate quantity of CQDs may slightly modify the crystal phase of BMO, which evidently requires further confirmation through additional characterization methods.

Raman spectroscopy provides information on molecular vibrations, enabling the identification of chemical bonds and functional groups within materials. As shown in Fig. 2(c), the translational vibrations of Mo and Bi atoms are always manifested below 200 cm−1 of Raman shift. Both BMO and C6BMO samples exhibit bands at 143 cm−1 and 189 cm−1, assigning to Bi3+ atoms' lattice vibrational modes predominantly in the direction perpendicular to the layers, which corresponds to stretching vibrations [47]. The introduction of CQDs may facilitate the formation of [Bi2O2]2+ layers, through observing a new characteristic peak emerged at 95 cm−1 [48]. Peaks near 281 cm−1 and 290 cm−1 are likely associated with the E g bending vibrational modes of MoO6 octahedra [49], while peaks at 324 cm−1, 347 cm−1 and 396 cm−1 correspond to the E u symmetric bending modes of MoO6 octahedra [50]. After the incorporation of CQDs, due to their inherently low Raman spectral intensity, the characteristic peak intensities of the modified samples decrease significantly [42]. When the 275 to 285 cm−1 range is magnified (Fig. 2(c) inset), almost all vibrational mode peaks exhibit noticeable shifts. Moreover, the E g mode peaks at 285 cm−1 and 296 cm−1 become more distinct in the modified samples, suggesting that the surface modification by CQDs during synthesis may influence the E g bending vibrational modes of MoO6 octahedra. These Raman analysis results indicate that the appropriate introduction of CQDs may slightly alter the composite samples' crystal structure. Notably, the primary characteristic peaks remain largely unchanged, in agreement with the results of XRD analysis.

FT-IR spectroscopy further confirms the functional groups and chemical bonds of CQDs/BMO nanocomposites. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2(d), FT-IR absorption bands in the ranges of 500 to 600 cm−1 and 700 to 950 cm−1 are attributed to the MoO bonds' stretching vibrations and BiO bonds' deformation/stretching modes [51,52]. The broad band around 3420 cm−1 associates with the surface hydroxyl groups existing on samples [20]. To further investigate the changes induced by CQDs modification, the 1500–1800 cm−1 range is magnified in Fig. 2(d). The composite samples exhibit significantly stronger characteristic vibrational peaks near 1580 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1 compared to the pure samples. These peaks are ascribed to the CQDs whose C–O–C and CO groups are on the composite sample surfaces [53,54]. The intensities of these peaks initially increase with the addition of trace amounts of CQDs, then decrease as the CQDs reach saturation. Apart from the aforementioned specific peak changes, other characteristic peaks of the composite samples exhibit minimal differences from the pure BMO samples. Therefore, the FT-IR analysis effectively demonstrates the surface modification effect of CQDs in the composite materials, which aligns well with the characterization results obtained from XRD and Raman spectroscopy.

Fig. 3 presents the physical object image of the BMO and C6BMO composite material with 6 wt% CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the sample primarily consists of densely packed flower-like microspheres with diameters varying from around 1 to 2 μm. Upon further magnification, these flower-like microspheres are revealed that their main components are actually a large amount of flat nanosheets with thicknesses of approximately 10–20 nm (Fig. 3(b)). The nanosheet surfaces and their surrounding areas are densely decorated with CQDs. Compared to other nanostructures (nanosheets, nanorods, etc.), This structure presents a greater exposed surface area, making it more advantageous for photocatalytic reactions [10]. As observed through comparative analysis, the surface and interlayer regions of pure BMO nanosheets exhibited smooth and clean morphological characteristics (Fig. 3(c-d)). The TEM image of C6BMO is displayed in Fig. 4 (a-b). This image distinctly shows numerous spherical, CQDs distributed across the nanosheet interfaces, along with irregular protrusions observed at the edges of the effective imaging area, which result from the random rearrangement of BMO nanosheets. After amplifying, it reveals that the observed CQDs exhibit a lattice spacing of 0.332 nm, aligning with the (002) plane. Meanwhile, the BMO's lattice spacing presents 0.316 nm, which associates with the (131) plane, slightly smaller than that of the CQDs (002) plane [54]. These findings evidently confirm the formation of the CQDs/BMO composite material. Furthermore, the elemental mapping images demonstrate the even distribution of Mo, Bi, O, and C elements within CQDs/BMO composite (Fig. 4(c-h)). The presence of carbon is attributed to the CQDs serving as the modifying agent, and its distribution is significantly more dispersed than that of the other elements.

The surface chemical compositions in CQDs, C6BMO composite, and pure BMO are analyzed using XPS. C9BMO is similarly analyzed for comparison. Mo, Bi, C, and O elements are revealed through XPS survey spectrum of the C6BMO sample, consistent with the results of TEM elemental mapping. The presence of O, C, and N elements are shown in the XPS survey spectrum of CQDs as well (Fig. 5(a)). The high-resolution XPS spectrum for CQDs is depicted in Fig. 5(b). In the C 1s region exhibits two signal peaks attributed to the CC bond (284.6 eV, used for calibration) and the N–C=N bond (287.04 eV) [55]. Correspondingly, the O 1 s spectrum shows two signal peaks at 530.9 eV and 532.91 eV, which are assigned to CO and CO bonds [56]. In the Bi 4f spectrum (Fig. 5(c)), the peaks at 158.7 eV and 164.1 eV in both BMO and composite samples correspond to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, which confirm the trivalent oxidation state of Bi [57]. The Mo 3d peaks (Fig. 5(d)) at approximately 232.0 and 235.2 eV are deconvoluted into two peaks due to the Mo6+ oxidation state, corresponding to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 [58]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 5(e)) of BMO can be deconvoluted into two peaks with binding energies at 529.59 eV and 531.18 eV, indicating the presence of lattice oxygen in the metal oxides (BiO, MoO) and adsorbed oxygen or surface hydroxyl groups on the catalyst [59]. After the incorporation of CQDs, the lattice oxygen peak of the metal oxide exhibits a slight left shift (<0.1 eV). Furthermore, the adsorbed oxygen peak of the C6BMO sample shifts significantly to the right by 0.5 eV because of the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on CQDs surface. For the C9BMO sample, although the addition of CQDs becomes saturated and the oxygen adsorption capability declines, the adsorbed oxygen peak still shifts by 0.21 eV. The BMO's high resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (Fig. 5(f)) reveals three peaks at binding energies of 284.6 eV (used for calibration), 286.13 eV, and 288.02 eV, corresponding to CC bonds, surface C–O–C bonds and CO bonds [53,54]. For C6BMO, the binding energy peak of C–O–C bonds (285.69 eV) appears more prominent and distinct compared to the pure material, and it shifts by 0.44 eV toward a lower potential. Similarly, the binding energy peak of CO bonds (285.69 eV) shifts to the right by 0.32 eV. For C9BMO, owing to excessive CQDs saturation, the difference compared to the pure material is notably smaller than that of C6BMO. These observations regarding carbon bonds in the C 1s XPS spectra align closely with the results obtained from FT-IR analysis.

To further validate the photoelectric performance of the samples, detailed EIS and photocurrent measurements were conducted. In Nyquist plots, the radius of the semicircle represents the impedance magnitude of the system at a given frequency. A smaller radius corresponds to lower charge transfer resistance, faster reaction kinetics and higher efficiency of electron or ion transport [60]. The CQDs/BMO composites exhibit much smaller semicircle radii compared to pure BMO (Fig. 6(a)). Using the ZsimpWin software, an equivalent circuit model was constructed, where CPE, Zw, Rct, and Rs stand for the constant phase element, Warburg impedance, charge transfer resistance, and electrolyte resistance [61]. Fitting results reveal an interface transfer resistance of 43.5 Ω for C6BMO, significantly lower than the 652.5 Ω observed for pure BMO. In photocurrent response measurements (Fig. 6(b)), the CQDs/BMO composite photocatalysts demonstrate a significantly higher photocurrent density compared to pure BMO, with C6BMO achieving a photocurrent density approximately 14 times that of pure BMO. Fig. 6(c) summarizes the above results, where the correlation between photocurrent and interface transfer resistance clearly underscores their interconnected influence on photoelectrical performance. The appropriate incorporation of CQDs can substantially inhibit photogenerated carriers' recombination, thereby benefiting the photocatalytic process by prolonging the lifetime of active particles. But excessive CQDs introduction leads to interface saturation, reducing photocatalytic performance. The PL spectroscopy analysis of all samples under 350 nm excited wavelength is shown in Fig. 6(d). With varying intensities, the prominent and asymmetric emission peak at 474 nm is observed for all samples. The emission intensity is closely related to the photogenerated carriers' recombination rate of detected catalysts. The most diminished peak intensity of C6BMO suggests that CQDs effectively suppress recombination for photogenerated carriers, optimizing photocatalytic efficiency mostly in the case of loading 6 wt% CQDs. The optimal performance of C6BMO aligns well with the conclusions drawn from the photoelectric tests.

The photocatalytic performances of all samples were systematically investigated by simulating H2O2 synthesis under light irradiation at room temperature (25 °C). Fig. 7(a) illustrates the time-dependent generation of H2O2 under visible light (λ > 400 nm). Ascribed to the in-situ reactions of H2O2 under illumination, resulting H2O2 yield does not exhibit a linear relationship with reaction time [51]. The following reactions describe the partial decomposition of H2O2:

Fig. 7(b) presents the H2O2 generation rates for each catalyst after 120 min of light irradiation, with the inset showing the results of three independent experiments. The average H2O2 production rates for BMO, C5BMO, C6BMO, C7BMO, and C9BMO are 23.15, 137.38, 272.18, 208.6, and 166.6 μmol g−1 h−1. Following the BMO's surface modification with CQDs, H2O2 production rates increase by 5.93 to 11.75 times compared to pure BMO. This enhancement is attributed to the CQDs enlarging the specific surface area [54], boosting the quantity of surface-active sites, providing additional reaction centers, thereby improving the reaction rate and H2O2 yield. Similarly, the H2O2 production rates under full-spectrum light irradiation (Xe lamp) are further tested (Fig. 7(c)), with the inset showing the time-dependent generation of H2O2. The average production rates for BMO, C5BMO, C6BMO, C7BMO, and C9BMO under full-spectrum light increase by 2.08, 1.40, 1.11, 1.31, and 1.43 times respectively, compared to their rates under visible light. The composite samples continue to exhibit significantly superior photocatalytic performance compared to pure BMO. Notably, C6BMO which demonstrates the best performance under visible light, exhibits minimal improvement under full-spectrum light, indicating its suitability for a wide range of light irradiation scenarios.

Based on above analysis, the generation of H2O2 is accompanied by decomposition behavior, inherently involving certain kinetic properties. Under anaerobic conditions, the decomposition rate of H2O2 under visible light irradiation at room temperature (initial concentration: 1 mmol L−1) is studied (Fig. 8(a)) for the purpose of probing the generation and decomposition kinetics during the samples' H2O2 production. The findings reveal that average decomposition percentage is 4.55 % for pure BMO, demonstrating the slowest decomposition rate, whereas C6BMO exhibited the highest decomposition rate at 11.8 % within 120 min. The decomposition percentages for C5BMO, C7BMO, and C9BMO are 7.7 %, 10.8 % and 9.07 %. The formation rate constant (K f, μmol g−1 min−1) and decomposition rate constant (K d, min−1) can be calculated through the following equations:

where C[H₂O₂] stands for the H2O2 concentration of the reaction solution and T denotes the reaction time [45]. For BMO, the K f and K d values are 0.1580 μmol g−1 min−1 and 3.885 ∗ 10−4 min−1. After incorporating an appropriate amount of CQDs, the K f values for C5BMO and C6BMO increase to 0.9531 and 1.9517 μmol g−1 min−1, while their K d values rise to 6.6795 and 10.5213 ∗ 10−4 min−1 respectively (Fig. 8(b)). This enhancement can be regarded as a result of the rich functional groups on CQDs, effectively suppressing the recombination of photogenerated carriers, which significantly improves production efficiency. As inferred from Eqs. (2) and (3), the exceptional behavioral capabilities of photogenerated carriers driven by CQDs enable more active particles to engage in the decomposition of H2O2 [12], thus increasing the decomposition efficiency. When excessive CQDs are introduced, the photocatalytic performance begins to decline. For C7BMO and C9BMO, the K f values drop to 1.4777 and 1.1760 μmol g−1 min−1, while the K d values decrease to 9.5289 and 7.9256 ∗ 10−4 min−1. This decline is attributed to the excessive surface modification covering some active adsorption sites [62], resulting in reduced H2O2 yield. Simultaneously, fewer electrons and holes participate in H2O2 decomposition, leading to decreased decomposition efficiency. In terms of H2O2 production capacity, the performance of the composite samples still significantly outperforms that of the pure BMO. The kinetic evaluation results are consistent with the EIS test conclusions. Stability is a crucial indicator to evaluate the performance of photocatalysts. As depicted in Fig. 8(c) inset, after 4 cycles of testing, the H2O2 yield for the composite sample C6BMO decreases by only 5.34 %. The XRD patterns prior to and following the tests remained similar (Fig. 8(c)), which indicates a high degree of lattice structure stability. This demonstrates the excellent stability of photocatalytic performance. Through comparison, it is found that the enhancement ratio of H2O2 production via photocatalysis in this work is better than other modified photocatalysts in the reports (see Table 1 ), although it is slightly lower than that of other complex structured catalysts. What's more, the performances are in strong agreement with the photocatalytic BPA degradation reported by our group previously [41]. These also confirm the superior photocatalytic capability and versatility of CQDs/BMO composites.

To further analyze the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) positions for BMO, C6BMO (moderate CQDs), and C9BMO (overloaded CQDs), several characterizations conducive to determining the band structure were carried out. XPS valence band spectra (Fig. 9(a)) reveals that E XPS,VB values for BMO, C6BMO, and C9BMO are 1.71, 1.82, and 1.61 eV. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra is utilized to analyze the optical absorption and band gap for all samples (Fig. 9(b)). Pure BMO shows an absorption edge near 500 nm, and the electronic excitation from VB to the CB causes a sharp absorption transition. After the introduction of CQDs, all composite samples present a significant increase of absorption rates in the visible light region, and their absorption edges exhibit a pronounced red shift. This phenomenon is attributed to the distinctive CQDs' up-conversion photoluminescence characteristics [64]. For semiconductors with direct band gaps, such as BMO or CQDs/BMO, the relationship can be expressed as:
(6)
αhυ
2
= K
hυ −
E g
where E g, ν, h, K, and α denote the band gap, photon frequency, Planck's constant, constant, and absorption coefficient [65]. As shown in the inset of Fig. 9(b), E g is calculated from the Tauc plots using above relationship. The E g of BMO is found to be 2.74 eV, in agreement with the reported value [51,52]. After incorporating CQDs, the red-shifted absorption edges result in reduced band gaps for CQDs/BMO (varying 2.5 to 2.68 eV), the light absorption ranges of optimized photocatalysts are considerably extended consequently. Mott-Schottky plots yields flat-band potentials (E fb) of −0.89, −0.4 and −0.45 V vs. RHE for three photocatalysts (Fig. 9(c)). The positive slopes observed in the Mott-Schottky plots demonstrate that all samples are n-type semiconductors, with the conduction band potential (E CB) closely matches E fb values [66]. E XPS,VB represents the energy difference of Fermi level (E f) and valence band potential (E VB) [11]. Using the following relationships, the band structures of BMO, C6BMO, and C9BMO can be constructed (Fig. 9(d)):

The E VB for BMO, C6BMO, and C9BMO are calculated to be 1.85, 2.11 and 2.05 V vs. RHE, with corresponding Fermi levels of 0.24, 0.29, and 0.34 V vs. RHE. Conspicuously the Fermi level of C6BMO is closer to the conduction band, indicating a higher free electron concentration and improved n-type semiconductor behavior.

The mechanism of photocatalytic H2O2 production generally involves two primary pathways: ORR (Eqs. (9) (10)) and WOR (Eqs. (11) (12) (13)) [67]. The corresponding reactions are as follows:
(9)
O 2
+ 2 e ⁻ + 2
H +
→ H ₂ O ₂
(10)
˙
O 2
−
+ e ⁻ + 2
H +
→ H ₂ O ₂
(11)
2 H ₂ O + 2
h +
→ H ₂ O ₂ + 2
H +
(12)
2 ˙ OH → H ₂ O ₂
(13)
2 H ₂ O + 4
h +
→
O 2
+ 4
H +

These reactions involve the generation of reactive radicals:
(14)
O 2
+ e ⁻ → ˙
O 2
−
(15)
OH −
+
h +
→ ˙ OH

The role of radicals as intermediates in ORR and WOR underscores their significance in photocatalytic processes. Radical capture experiments were carried out using the C6BMO sample to explore the role of radicals in the photocatalytic process. BQ, IPA, AO, and K2Cr2O7 are introduced to selectively capture O2 −, OH, h+, and e− during the photocatalytic reaction [42,68,69]. Fig. 10(a) inset illustrates the changes in H2O2 yield over time after the addition of different scavengers. The yield bar chart (Fig. 10(a)) provides a more intuitive representation of these data. When IPA and AO are added to capture OH and h+, the average yields from three independent experiments decrease by approximately 34.88 % and 60.77 %. This result indicates that OH and h+ play pivotal roles in the WOR, with h+ contributing more significantly due to its involvement in Eqs. (11), (13) and (15). Consequently, capturing h+ led to a more pronounced decline in H2O2 yield. Upon the addition of BQ and K2Cr2O7, which capture O2 − and e−, the H2O2 yield exhibits a sharp decline. The average yields from three independent experiments decrease substantially by 85.08 % and 90.18 %. This finding underscores the critical roles of O2 − and e−, which are as the key reactive intermediates during ORR photocatalytic process. The experimental atmosphere was changed through continuous purging with O2 or N2 to further investigate the photocatalytic performance variations in C6BMO. As observed in the results of Fig. 10(b), H2O2 production increased by approximately 40 % under oxygen-rich conditions, whereas N2 purging reduced yields by nearly one-third. These findings suggest that molecular oxygen, particularly dissolved oxygen, serves as a critical reactant in the ORR, and electron utilization efficiency is significantly enhanced under oxygen-enriched environments, leading to substantially improved H2O2 generation. In order to eliminate potential interference factors, an equivalent mass of physically mixed CQDs and BMO (BMO&CQDs) was uniformly dispersed in the solvent for photocatalytic testing as a control experiment. The mixture exhibits marginally higher H2O2 yields compared to pure BMO, which may be attributed to limited interfacial interactions between CQDs and BMO or minor intrinsic photocatalytic activity of CQDs. However, the enhancement remains markedly inferior to that achieved by the synthesized C6BMO composite, confirming the structural superiority of C6BMO. We also conducted H2O2 production experiments with the same mass of CQDs under identical experimental conditions. The results indicate that the average production rate of CQDs is only 10.23 μmol g−1 h−1, which strongly suggests that their individual contribution to the photocatalytic activity is negligible when used alone. This finding confirms that CQDs must be incorporated into composite materials to exhibit significant functionality. The 40 % yield enhancement under O2 saturation implies optimized electron transfer kinetics in C6BMO, contrasting sharply with the limited activity of physically mixed components. This performance gap underscores the critical role of engineered interfacial architectures in photocatalytic systems. EPR spectroscopy results (Fig. 10(c)) further corroborate these observations. Under dark conditions, the solution exhibits negligible radical content. Upon light irradiation to simulate the photocatalytic process, the concentration of O2 − significantly surpasses that of OH. As intermediates in ORR and WOR, these results align well with the scavenger experiments, leading to the conclusion that the photocatalytic production of H2O2 in this study is predominantly governed by the ORR. What's more, as an n-type semiconductor, BMO demonstrates superior capability in facilitating reductive reactions, thereby providing further evidence for the dominant role of the ORR in the system. It is noteworthy that, according to Eq. (13), WOR generates O2 and H+, which serve as initial substrates for ORR, indirectly promoting the ORR. In contrast, ORR does not produce substances that favor WOR, further substantiating the dominance of the ORR. In addition, even though OH has the smallest impact, it still reduces the H2O2 yield by approximately one-third, highlighting the non-negligible contribution of WOR to the photocatalytic activity.

Based on the analysis of band theory and radical detection experiments, a possible mechanism for photocatalytic synthesis of H2O2 is put forward (Fig. 11 ). Under simulated solar radiation, the CQDs/BMO composite efficiently absorbs solar light, generating a significant quantity of photogenerated carriers. The CQDs incorporated onto the BMO surface function by trapping electrons and supplying active sites, aiding in the swift e− migration from the interior of semiconductor to its surface, which effectively separates photogenerated e−– h+ pairs. Remarkably, the production potentials of H₂O₂ (1.14 and 1.44 V vs. RHE) lie within the E CB and E VB for catalyst, confirming its capacity to produce H2O2. The oxygen reduction potential (−0.33 V vs. RHE) is more positive than the CB potentials of catalysts, allowing plenty of e− migrating to the fitting place of reacting with adsorbed O2 and obtaining O2 − by means of oxygen reductions. Concurrently, O2 − reacts with H+ and e− through the ORR, yielding substantial amounts of H2O2. The VB of CQDs/BMO is larger than the potential required for h+ to oxidize OH− into OH (1.99 V vs. RHE), enabling the smooth progression of WOR and generating H2O2 by OH synthesis. In contrast, pure BMO has a VB lower than the oxidation potential for OH generation, leading to incomplete WOR. For C9BMO, modified with excessive CQDs, its band structure is altered. The excessive surface modification agents cover certain active adsorption sites, reducing the accessibility of oxygen and sacrificial agents, thereby diminishing photocatalytic performance. Although its VB still meets the requirements for OH generation, the CB of both C9BMO and pure BMO is farther from the oxygen reduction potential (−0.33 V vs. RHE) compared to C6BMO. Furthermore, the Fermi level of these materials is farther from the CB, resulting in fewer electrons available for reactions and reduced production of O2 −. In the photocatalytic processes dominated by ORR, it leads to a significant performance gap between these two materials and C6BMO.

In summary, highly efficient and practical CQDs/BMO composite photocatalysts with varying CQDs content were fabricated via a hydrothermal approach. Photocatalytic experiments and various characterization techniques indicate that the optimal state for photocatalytic H2O2 production is achieved when the CQDs loading is 6 wt%. At this composition, the morphology, structure, photoelectrochemical properties, and energy band alignment reach their peak performance. This enhancement arises from the inimitable synergistic interactions of CQDs, which significantly extends the light absorption range of BMO and effectively prevents photogenerated carriers from recombining. On the other hand, excessive CQDs modification may cover active adsorption sites, leading to adverse effects. The optimized catalyst facilitates the progress of both the WOR and ORR. In particular, WOR takes advantages of the enhanced production of hydroxyl radicals and accelerates ORR process indirectly. Despite its remarkably simple structure, the product of this study achieves over a tenfold enhancement in photocatalytic H2O2 production activity, far surpassing similar catalyst optimization strategies. This advancement offers an inventive paradigm for the progress of composite photocatalysts in related future research fields.
